does: This paper introduces LLM-as-an-Investigator, an evidence-first agentic methodology that addresses user-driven sycophancy in technical problem diagnosis. The core contribution is a Solution Investigator Agent that systematically gathers evidence before committing to solutions. The agent first estimates ambiguity in problem descriptions, generates multiple candidate hypotheses, and asks targeted clarification questions designed to discriminate between explanations. After each user answer, the agent updates hypothesis probabilities using Bayesian reasoning and continues investigation until one candidate achieves sufficient confidence relative to alternatives. Rather than immediately proposing fixes based on initial user statements, the approach enforces disciplined evidence collection, ensuring the LLM resists premature alignment with unverified user assumptions. The paper evaluates this methodology using a benchmark constructed from solved technical forum threads, measuring whether the agent's eventual diagnosis aligns with the ground-truth solutions.
connects: This work extends recent research on LLM reasoning quality and agent architectures by addressing a specific failure mode: confirmation bias and user-driven sycophancy in conversational AI systems. It builds on prior work on hypothesis testing in language models and connects to broader literature on structured reasoning in LLMs, particularly methodologies like chain-of-thought prompting and reasoning frameworks that impose discipline on model inference. The paper engages with interactive problem-solving systems and dialogue-based diagnosis, drawing from classical AI approaches to diagnostic systems that use iterative evidence collection and Bayesian belief updating. It also relates to work on robustness in LLM outputs and user interaction patterns, responding to observed failures where models too readily adopt user-provided framings rather than critically evaluating explanations.
enables: This work enables more reliable interactive technical support systems by establishing evidence-first reasoning as a paradigm for LLM-based problem diagnosis. It points toward broader applications of this investigative methodology in domains requiring rigorous hypothesis evaluation—customer service, debugging assistance, medical triage, and scientific investigation. The approach creates opportunities for studying how LLMs can maintain skepticism toward user-provided information while remaining collaborative. Future work could extend this to domains with higher stakes, develop methods for handling contradictory evidence, improve the efficiency of questioning strategies, and create hybrid systems combining LLMs with formal diagnostic tools. The benchmark contribution enables further research into evaluating diagnosis quality and measuring user-driven sycophancy as a systematic phenomenon requiring mitigation.
